nosql-001.prompt: Which mappings between a NoSQL category and its central characteristic are correct?

a.text: Key-value stores typically treat the value as an opaque byte sequence from the DBMS perspective.
a.explanation: The semantics of the value are primarily handled by the application.

b.text: Document stores can understand and filter fields within a document.
b.explanation: This is precisely what distinguishes them from pure key-value stores.

c.text: Graph databases treat relationships only as derived metadata without their own type or properties.
c.explanation: Relationships are first-class citizens and can have both a type and properties.

d.text: Wide-column stores typically address values using more than a single simple key.
d.explanation: Bigtable uses a row key, column, and timestamp.

nosql-001.explanation: The categories differ in which structure the DBMS understands and optimizes for access.

nosql-002.prompt: Which statements about aggregates in NoSQL systems are correct?

a.text: An aggregate can be the unit of access, update, and distribution.
a.explanation: This is the central idea of aggregate orientation.

b.text: Atomic changes to one aggregate are often easier than atomic changes across multiple aggregates.
b.explanation: Aggregate boundaries frequently also form transaction boundaries.

c.text: Once chosen, an aggregate boundary is equally optimal for every workload.
c.explanation: Different access patterns can favor conflicting boundaries.

d.text: Graph databases are typically considered aggregate-ignorant.
d.explanation: Much of their information lies in relationships between small entities.

nosql-002.explanation: Aggregates optimize data used together but create trade-offs for other access paths.

nosql-003.prompt: Which statements about sharding and replication are correct?

a.text: Sharding distributes different data ranges across multiple nodes.
a.explanation: It roughly corresponds to horizontal fragmentation.

b.text: Replication maintains multiple copies of the same data.
b.explanation: Replicas improve fault tolerance and read performance, among other benefits.

c.text: Primary-secondary replication normally allows every secondary to write independently to the same object.
c.explanation: Writes are processed through the primary.

d.text: Peer-to-peer replication can create additional write-write conflicts.
d.explanation: Concurrent writes on different nodes can conflict.

nosql-003.explanation: Sharding and replication solve different problems and can be used together.

a.text: Basically Available means that the system appears essentially available.
a.explanation: This is BA in BASE.

b.text: Soft State permits replicas to temporarily have different states.
b.explanation: The state does not have to be fully consistent at all times.

c.text: Eventual Consistency guarantees a fixed maximum convergence time.
c.explanation: A specific time bound is not part of the guarantee.

d.text: If no further updates occur, replicas should eventually converge.
d.explanation: That is exactly what eventual consistency means.

nosql-004.explanation: BASE deliberately provides weaker, distribution-friendly guarantees than full ACID.

nosql-005.prompt: Which statements use the CAP terms according to the lecture definitions?

a.text: Availability requires a response to every request received by a non-failing node.
a.explanation: This is the precise definition used in the slides.

b.text: Consistency means single-copy consistency.
b.explanation: The system behaves like a single copy processing operations sequentially.

c.text: Partition Tolerance means that a network partition must never occur.
c.explanation: The system is meant to continue operating despite a partition.

d.text: A system is available in the CAP sense as soon as any one cluster node is running.
d.explanation: That is much weaker than CAP availability.

nosql-005.explanation: Many CAP mistakes arise from imprecise everyday definitions of availability and consistency.

nosql-006.prompt: Which conclusions from CAP and PACELC are correct?

a.text: Because network partitions cannot be ruled out reliably, AP versus CP is the practically relevant CAP decision.
a.explanation: A CA system would stop operating during a partition.

b.text: PACELC additionally considers the trade-off between latency and consistency when no partition exists.
b.explanation: This is the EL/C part.

c.text: PA/EC favors consistency during a partition and latency otherwise.
c.explanation: PA means availability during a partition; EC means consistency in the else case.

d.text: CAP requires a system to permanently choose exactly two of the three properties, regardless of operating conditions.
d.explanation: The critical trade-off applies during a partition, and the properties are not purely binary.

nosql-006.explanation: PACELC separates the partition case from normal operation.

nosql-007.prompt: A system has replication factor N = 7. Which (R,W) pairs satisfy both quorum conditions?
nosql-007.context: The required conditions are 2W > N and R + W > N.

a.explanation: 2W=8>7, but R+W=7 is not greater than 7.

d.explanation: R+W=8, but 2W=4 is not greater than 7.

nosql-007.explanation: Both inequalities must be checked independently.

nosql-008.prompt: Which statements about quorum consensus are correct?

a.text: ROWA uses R = 1 and W = N.
a.explanation: Read One, Write All.

b.text: The condition R + W > N guarantees that a read reaches at least one replica involved in the latest write.
b.explanation: The read and write quorums must intersect.

c.text: The condition 2W > N prevents two write quorums from being disjoint.
c.explanation: Two sets that each contain more than N/2 elements overlap.

d.text: Version stamps are fundamentally unnecessary when the quorum conditions are satisfied.
d.explanation: The newest version still has to be identified within a read quorum.

nosql-008.explanation: Quorums ensure overlap; versioning identifies the current value.

nosql-009.prompt: Which rules for Lamport clocks are correct?

a.text: Before sending a message, the sender increments its local counter.
a.explanation: The incremented value is attached to the message.

b.text: When receiving timestamp t, the local counter is set to max(local,t)+1.
b.explanation: This places the receive logically after the send.

c.text: Equal Lamport timestamps on two processes prove that they represent the same event.
c.explanation: Independent events can have the same scalar value.

d.text: C(e) < C(e') always implies e → e'.
d.explanation: Lamport clocks satisfy only the implication in the other direction.

nosql-009.explanation: Lamport timestamps respect causality but do not capture it completely.

nosql-010.prompt: Which values result from this Lamport-clock sequence?
nosql-010.context: Initially, A=2 and B=5. A sends m1 to B. B then sends m2 to A. There are no other events.

a.text: m1 carries timestamp 3.
a.explanation: A increments before sending: 2→3.

b.text: After receiving m1, B is at 6.

c.text: m2 carries timestamp 7.
c.explanation: B increments before sending: 6→7.

d.text: After receiving m2, A is at 7.

nosql-010.explanation: Every send increments first; every receive uses the maximum plus one.

nosql-011.prompt: Which statements about the weak and strong clock properties are correct?

a.text: Lamport clocks satisfy e→e' ⇒ C(e)<C(e').
a.explanation: This is the weak clock property.

b.text: Lamport clocks satisfy C(e)<C(e') ⇒ e→e'.
b.explanation: Independent event sequences can receive ordered scalar timestamps.

c.text: Vector clocks satisfy e→e' if and only if VC(e)<VC(e').
c.explanation: They satisfy the strong clock property.

d.text: Concurrent events necessarily receive identical vectors with vector clocks.
d.explanation: They typically receive incomparable, non-identical vectors.

nosql-011.explanation: The key advantage of vector clocks is that they reveal causality and concurrency.

nosql-012.prompt: Which comparisons of the following vector clocks are correct?
nosql-012.context: x=(3,2,5), y=(3,4,5), z=(4,1,6), w=(3,4,6). Comparison is component-wise.

a.text: x < y
a.explanation: Every component of x is ≤ the corresponding component of y, and the second is strictly smaller.

b.text: y < w
b.explanation: Only the third component increases from 5 to 6.

c.text: x < z
c.explanation: x has 2>1 in the second component, so the vectors are incomparable.

d.text: z and w are incomparable.
d.explanation: z is greater in component 1, while w is greater in component 2.

nosql-012.explanation: For <, every component must be ≤ and at least one must be strictly smaller. Crossing components mean incomparability.

nosql-013.prompt: Process B with vector clock (2,5,1) receives a message from A carrying (4,3,2). Which statements are correct?

a.text: After taking the component-wise maximum but before the local increment, B is at (4,5,2).
a.explanation: The maximum per component is (4,5,2).

b.text: After completing the receive operation, B is at (4,6,2).
b.explanation: B then increments its own second component.

c.text: B must increment its first component because the message came from A.
c.explanation: The receiver always increments its own component.

d.text: The new vector is smaller than the received vector.
d.explanation: It is component-wise greater than or equal, and strictly greater in B's component.

nosql-013.explanation: Receive operation: first take the component-wise maximum, then increment the receiver's component.

nosql-014.prompt: Which mappings between a session guarantee and its meaning are correct?

a.text: Read-Your-Writes: after its own write, a session reads at least that version.
a.explanation: A session's own writes do not disappear from its view.

b.text: Monotonic Reads: a session never later reads an older version than one it has already read.
b.explanation: The versions read by the session move monotonically forward.

c.text: Monotonic Writes: writes from the same session may be observed by others in any order.
c.explanation: The guarantee is specifically intended to preserve their order.

d.text: Writes-Follow-Reads preserves causal dependencies between a read and a subsequent write.
d.explanation: Anyone who sees the later write should also see the state read before it, or a newer state.

nosql-014.explanation: These guarantees are concrete forms of causal or session-related consistency.

nosql-015.prompt: A system has replication factor N = 8. Which of the following quorum configurations satisfy both conditions?
nosql-015.context: The required conditions are 2W > N and R + W > N. It is possible that no option is correct.

a.explanation: 2W=8 is not strictly greater than 8, and R+W is only 8.

b.explanation: R+W=9 is sufficient, but 2W=8 is not strictly greater than 8.

c.explanation: 2W=10 is sufficient, but R+W=8 is not strictly greater than 8.

d.explanation: 2W=14 is sufficient, but R+W=8 is not strictly greater than 8.

nosql-015.explanation: No option is correct. With even N, the strict inequalities are a common exam trap.

kv-001.prompt: Which statements typically characterize key-value stores correctly?

a.text: Values are addressed by keys.
a.explanation: This is the fundamental access model.

b.text: The internal semantics of a value are typically known only to the application.
b.explanation: The store largely treats values as byte sequences.

c.text: Complex ad-hoc joins are part of the typical core access pattern.
c.explanation: Point lookups by key usually dominate.

d.text: Many systems are optimized for high write rates and scalability.
d.explanation: These are central goals of this category.

kv-001.explanation: Key-value stores simplify the query model in favor of speed and scalability.

kv-002.prompt: Which statements about Bitcask's file organization are correct?

a.text: At any time, exactly one data file is active and writable.
a.explanation: All new entries are appended to that file.

b.text: When an entry is updated, closed files are overwritten at the corresponding position.
b.explanation: Closed files are immutable.

c.text: A delete is represented by an appended tombstone.
c.explanation: Deletions are append-only as well.

d.text: An update creates a new version of the entry.
d.explanation: The older version remains physically present until the merge.

kv-002.explanation: Bitcask performs all changes as sequential appends.

kv-003.prompt: Which information does Bitcask's keydir store for every live key?

a.text: file_id
a.explanation: It identifies the data file.

b.text: Offset and value size
b.explanation: They enable a targeted disk read.

c.text: The complete value itself
c.explanation: The value remains in the data file.

d.text: Timestamp of the newest version
d.explanation: It is part of the metadata structure.

kv-003.explanation: The keydir keeps keys and fixed-size metadata in RAM, not all values.

kv-004.prompt: A Bitcask processes put(k,A), put(k,B), delete(k), and put(k,C) in that order. Which statements are then correct?

a.text: The files physically contain four appended entries for k.
a.explanation: Two puts, one tombstone, and another put were appended.

b.text: The keydir points to the entry containing C.
b.explanation: It is the newest live version.

c.text: The tombstone must remain permanently in the keydir.
c.explanation: Deleting removes k from the keydir; the later put adds it again.

d.text: Get(k) can read C with at most one targeted disk access.
d.explanation: The keydir provides the file, offset, and size.

kv-004.explanation: Physical history and the current logical state are separate in append-only stores.

kv-005.prompt: Which statements about Bitcask merging and hint files are correct?

a.text: The merge processes inactive data files.
a.explanation: The active file remains available for ongoing writes.

b.text: Obsolete versions and keys deleted by newer tombstones can be removed.
b.explanation: This reduces space amplification.

c.text: Hint files contain complete values in compressed form.
c.explanation: They contain no values, only position and size information.

d.text: After the merge, affected keydir entries must point to new positions.
d.explanation: The live values were written to new files.

kv-005.explanation: Merging cleans up history; hint files accelerate startup and recovery.

kv-006.prompt: Which statements about Bitcask's strengths and weaknesses are correct?

a.text: Writes are predominantly sequential appends.
a.explanation: This enables high throughput.

b.text: The number of keys can grow arbitrarily, independently of available RAM.
b.explanation: All keys and keydir metadata reside in RAM.

c.text: Point queries are very efficient.
c.explanation: They require the keydir and at most one disk read.

d.text: Range queries are a natural and efficient core access pattern in Bitcask.
d.explanation: Bitcask essentially supports point queries.

kv-006.explanation: Bitcask trades RAM usage and limited query types for very simple, fast operations.

kv-007.prompt: Which components of a LevelDB-style LSM tree reside in RAM?

a.text: Mutable memtable
a.explanation: It is the entry point for new reads and writes.

b.text: Immutable memtable while it is being flushed
b.explanation: It temporarily remains an in-memory structure.

c.text: Every SSTable from every level in full
c.explanation: SSTables reside on disk; only parts such as indexes may be cached.

d.text: Only the write-ahead log
d.explanation: The WAL must be persisted on disk.

kv-007.explanation: Memtables are volatile; the WAL and SSTables provide persistence.

kv-008.prompt: Which statements correctly distinguish flushing from compaction?

a.text: A flush writes an immutable memtable as an SSTable to level 0.
a.explanation: This is the transition from RAM to disk.

b.text: Compaction merges SSTables and removes obsolete versions.
b.explanation: Data are often moved to the next level in the process.

c.text: A flush immediately guarantees non-overlapping key ranges in level 0.
c.explanation: Level 0 may contain overlapping SSTables.

d.text: Compaction reduces read and space amplification but increases write amplification.
d.explanation: Data have to be read and written again.

kv-008.explanation: Flushing persists new data; compaction reorganizes data that are already persistent.

kv-009.prompt: Which statements about leveling and tiering are correct?

a.text: Leveling keeps SSTable key ranges non-overlapping within higher levels.
a.explanation: A point lookup therefore checks at most one SSTable per level.

b.text: Tiering permits overlapping SSTables and thus typically favors writes.
b.explanation: Less immediate merging reduces write cost.

c.text: Leveling fundamentally causes less write amplification than tiering.
c.explanation: Leveling typically rewrites data more often.

d.text: Tiering can increase read amplification.
d.explanation: Several overlapping files may be relevant.

kv-009.explanation: Leveling is more read-friendly, while tiering is more write-friendly.

kv-010.prompt: Which order correctly describes an LSM get operation?

a.text: Mutable memtable, immutable memtable, then SSTables starting at level 0.
a.explanation: The search proceeds from the newest to older layers.

b.text: At level 0, multiple SSTables may be checked from newest to oldest when necessary.
b.explanation: Their key ranges may overlap.

c.text: At every level above 0, all SSTables must always be read.
c.explanation: With non-overlapping ranges, at most one can contain the key.

d.text: A tombstone that is found means logically 'not present' and ends the search.
d.explanation: The newest version is a deletion.

kv-010.explanation: The search order must prioritize the most recent versions.

kv-011.prompt: Which statements about Bloom filters in LSM trees are correct?

a.text: A negative result proves that the key is not in this SSTable.
a.explanation: Bloom filters have no false negatives.

b.text: A positive result proves that the key is present.
b.explanation: False positives are possible.

c.text: Bloom filters help avoid unnecessary SSTable accesses.
c.explanation: Many files can be ruled out before a disk read.

d.text: Bloom filters completely replace the sorted block index.
d.explanation: After a possible match, the relevant block is located using structures such as fence pointers.

kv-011.explanation: Bloom filters answer either 'definitely no' or 'possibly yes'.

kv-012.prompt: An SSTable's Bloom filter returns a negative result even though the requested key is actually stored there. Which statements are correct?

a.text: This is a permitted false negative of a normal Bloom filter.
a.explanation: Normal Bloom filters have no false negatives.

b.text: The Bloom filter or its use is faulty.
b.explanation: An element that is present must not test negative.

c.text: A higher false-positive rate could explain this behavior.
c.explanation: False positives concern absent elements that produce a positive result.

d.text: The store could incorrectly skip the SSTable and return a wrong read result.
d.explanation: This is exactly why the no-false-negative property is necessary.

kv-012.explanation: A negative Bloom-filter result is used as a definitive exclusion.

kv-013.prompt: Which statements about an LSM range scan over [kmin,kmax] are correct?

a.text: Relevant memtables must be considered.
a.explanation: They may contain the newest versions.

b.text: Every SSTable whose key range overlaps the interval may contain relevant entries.
b.explanation: It must be included in the merge.

c.text: A merge-like evaluation determines the newest version for each key.
c.explanation: Older values and tombstones must be resolved correctly.

d.text: The ordering of SSTables is useless for range queries.
d.explanation: It enables efficient iterators and merge processing.

kv-013.explanation: Range scans are possible, but their data are distributed across multiple sorted, time-layered sources.

kv-014.prompt: Which comparisons among Bitcask, LSM trees, and B+ trees are correct?

a.text: Bitcask is typically simpler and more direct than an LSM tree for point queries.
a.explanation: The keydir plus one disk read is very efficient.

b.text: LSM trees support range queries better than log-structured hash tables.
b.explanation: Keys and SSTables are sorted.

c.text: LSM trees achieve high write rates partly through sequential writes and memtable buffering.
c.explanation: This is their main strength compared with B+ trees.

d.text: LSM trees avoid every form of background I/O.
d.explanation: Flushing and especially compaction cause substantial background work.

kv-014.explanation: The structures optimize different workloads; no design dominates every access pattern.

doc-001.prompt: Which statements distinguish document stores from pure key-value stores?

a.text: A document store typically understands fields inside JSON, XML, or BSON documents.
a.explanation: This enables structure-aware queries.

b.text: A document store can filter by field values.
b.explanation: This is one of its extended query capabilities.

c.text: A key-value store must never store JSON as a value.
c.explanation: It can store JSON but typically does not understand its structure.

d.text: Document stores always require an identical rigid schema for every document.
d.explanation: Flexible document structures are a typical characteristic.

doc-001.explanation: The decisive factor is not the file format alone, but whether the DBMS understands the structure.

doc-002.prompt: Which principles of document modeling are correct?

a.text: The expected access pattern should influence the model.
a.explanation: Document models are workload-oriented.

b.text: Data that must be changed atomically together should preferably reside in one document.
b.explanation: Single-document operations form the natural atomicity boundary.

c.text: The maximum BSON document size can be ignored when arrays are used.
c.explanation: Arrays are also part of the document, whose maximum size is 16 MB.

d.text: Index decisions are part of data modeling.
d.explanation: They follow from the intended access patterns.

doc-002.explanation: Accesses, updates, atomicity, growth, validation, and indexes must be considered together.

doc-003.prompt: Which modeling decisions match the cardinalities discussed in the lecture?

a.text: One-to-few: store dependent objects as an embedded array in the one-side document.
a.explanation: Small sets that are read together fit well in one document.

b.text: One-to-millions: always embed millions of subdocuments in a single array.
b.explanation: Document growth and the 16 MB limit favor separate collections.

c.text: One-to-thousands: a list of references on the one side can be suitable.
c.explanation: This is one of the variants presented in the slides.

d.text: One-to-millions: separate collections with a reference from the many side are typically suitable.
d.explanation: This keeps the one-side document bounded.

doc-003.explanation: Cardinality is an important decision factor, but not the only one.

doc-004.prompt: A customer has up to 30 orders. The customer and recent orders are almost always read together, and orders are rarely changed after completion. Which statements are plausible?

a.text: Embedding the orders can reduce the frequent combined query to one document read.
a.explanation: The access pattern favors embedding.

b.text: Single-document updates to the customer and an embedded order can be atomic.
b.explanation: Both pieces of data lie within the same atomicity boundary.

c.text: The solution is always optimal, regardless of document growth and the 16 MB limit.
c.explanation: Size and growth still have to be checked.

d.text: If there were millions of orders with frequent individual updates, references would be more suitable.
d.explanation: An unbounded embedded array would then be problematic.

doc-004.explanation: Embedding is a workload-specific optimization, not a universal rule.

doc-005.prompt: Which statements about denormalization are correct?

a.text: It redundantly copies data to avoid additional reads or joins.
a.explanation: That is its main purpose.

b.text: It automatically makes updates across multiple documents atomic.
b.explanation: Redundancy actually aggravates the problem of non-atomic multi-document updates.

c.text: It is especially attractive when the read-to-write ratio is high.
c.explanation: Many fast reads can justify rare, expensive updates.

d.text: Denormalizing one-side data into the many side can spread one change across many documents.
d.explanation: This can greatly increase update costs.

doc-005.explanation: Denormalization trades consistency and update effort for faster reads.

doc-006.prompt: A relational many-to-many Student–Lecture relationship is modeled using two collections. Each student stores lecture IDs, each lecture stores student IDs, and the actual exam data are stored separately. Which statements are correct?

a.text: Neighborhood access from either side can avoid scanning the other collection.
a.explanation: Both document types contain reference lists.

b.text: Adding a relationship requires consistent changes to at least two documents.
b.explanation: Both reference lists must be updated.

c.text: An empty reference list [] is semantically identical to a missing field.
c.explanation: [] represents an existing but empty relationship list; a missing field may mean something else.

d.text: This model eliminates all redundancy.
d.explanation: The relationship is represented redundantly on both sides.

doc-006.explanation: Bidirectional references speed up reads but increase maintenance effort.

doc-007.prompt: Which statements about MongoDB replica sets are correct?

a.text: Writes are executed on the primary.
a.explanation: The primary is the write entry point.

b.text: Secondaries replay the primary's operations from logs in the same order.
b.explanation: This is how replication is performed.

c.text: An arbiter stores a full data copy but cannot vote.
c.explanation: The reverse is true: it votes but stores no data.

d.text: If the primary fails, secondaries can hold an election.
d.explanation: A new primary can be elected.

doc-007.explanation: Replica sets combine primary-secondary replication with automatic election.

doc-008.prompt: Which statements about MongoDB read preferences are correct?

a.text: primary is the default preference.
a.explanation: Reads go to the primary by default.

b.text: secondaryPreferred reads from a secondary if possible and otherwise from the primary.
b.explanation: This is its fallback semantics.

c.text: nearest necessarily selects the server with the most recent data.
c.explanation: Network latency, not freshness, is decisive.

d.text: Reads from secondaries may return stale data.
d.explanation: Replication is not necessarily synchronous.

doc-008.explanation: Read preference determines the target node and affects latency, load, and possible staleness.

doc-009.prompt: Which statements correctly distinguish write concern, read concern, and read preference?

a.text: Write concern w:2 requires acknowledgements from two servers.
a.explanation: A concrete number can be specified.

b.text: j:true requires the write to be recorded in the on-disk journal.
b.explanation: This strengthens the durability guarantee.

c.text: Read concern majority and read preference secondary are identical settings.
c.explanation: Concern determines the required view; preference determines the read target.

d.text: Read concern local reads the current value of the selected read target.
d.explanation: This is the default read concern.

doc-009.explanation: Where a read occurs and which version is acceptable are separate decisions.

doc-010.prompt: Which statements about MongoDB transactions are correct?

a.text: Single-document operations are atomic even without an explicit multi-document transaction.
a.explanation: This is the preferred, fast atomicity boundary.

b.text: Multi-document ACID transactions have existed since MongoDB 4.0.
b.explanation: They were introduced in 2018.

c.text: With transactions, choosing suitable document boundaries becomes irrelevant.
c.explanation: Single-document operations remain faster and simpler.

d.text: A session can commit or abort a transaction.
d.explanation: commitTransaction and abortTransaction are provided.

doc-010.explanation: MongoDB supports ACID across multiple documents but still recommends aggregate-appropriate modeling.

doc-011.prompt: Which components belong to a MongoDB sharded cluster?

a.text: At least two shards
a.explanation: The slides specify two or more.

b.text: One or more mongos query routers
b.explanation: They route requests.

c.text: Config servers configured as a replica set
c.explanation: They store cluster metadata and configuration.

d.text: Every shard must be a single, non-replicated file.
d.explanation: A shard is operated as a replica set.

doc-011.explanation: Sharding and replication are nested within each other in MongoDB.

doc-012.prompt: Given db.books.find({price: {$gte: 10, $lte: 20}}, {title: true, _id: false}), which statements are correct?

a.text: It searches for books priced between 10 and 20 inclusive.
a.explanation: $gte and $lte are inclusive.

b.text: The result includes title.
b.explanation: title:true includes the field in the projection.

c.text: The _id field is suppressed.
c.explanation: _id:false explicitly excludes it.

d.text: The query modifies the matching documents.
d.explanation: find is a read operation.

doc-012.explanation: The first document is the filter, and the second is the projection.

doc-013.prompt: Which statements about MongoDB updates are correct?

a.text: $set can change an existing field or add a new one.
a.explanation: It sets the specified field value.

b.text: $unset removes a field.
b.explanation: The field is deleted from the document.

c.text: updateMany({}, {$inc:{x:1}}) increments x in every document in the collection.
c.explanation: The empty query matches every document.

d.text: updateOne is guaranteed to update every matching document.
d.explanation: It updates only one matching document.

doc-013.explanation: The query, update operator, and one/many variant determine the scope and effect.

doc-014.prompt: A system must return the names of all guests who checked in during a given year. Bookings change frequently, while guest names change very rarely. Which design statements are defensible?

a.text: Storing guest_name and a derived year directly in each booking can support the target query without a join.
a.explanation: This deliberately denormalizes for the frequent read.

b.text: A rare guest rename may require updates to multiple booking documents.
b.explanation: This is the cost of redundant names.

c.text: Because bookings are written frequently, every form of denormalization is automatically invalid.
c.explanation: The concrete trade-off may still be worthwhile; the extra fields are small and the query is important.

d.text: An index on year can support filtering.
d.explanation: The derived field creates a direct, indexable access path.

doc-014.explanation: Good document models optimize the concrete workload and state their update costs explicitly.

graph-001.prompt: Which statements correctly describe the property-graph model?

a.text: Nodes can have labels.
a.explanation: Labels express roles in the application domain.

b.text: Relationships are named and directed.
b.explanation: Type and direction are core components.

c.text: Only nodes, but not relationships, can carry properties.
c.explanation: Both can have key-value properties.

d.text: Relationships are treated as first-class citizens.
d.explanation: They are central, directly stored data objects.

graph-001.explanation: Property graphs model nodes and relationships explicitly and enrich both with properties.

graph-002.prompt: Which transformations from a relational model to a graph model are correct?

a.text: An entity table typically becomes a node label.
a.explanation: Each row then becomes a node with that label.

b.text: A foreign key can be replaced by a relationship.
b.explanation: The relationship becomes explicit.

c.text: A many-to-many junction table can become a relationship whose additional columns become relationship properties.
c.explanation: This is the natural transformation.

d.text: Technical primary keys must always remain visible node properties.
d.explanation: The slides recommend removing purely technical primary keys.

graph-002.explanation: The graph model removes relational navigation artifacts and makes relationships direct.

graph-003.prompt: Which statements about Neo4j are correct according to the lecture?

a.text: Writes always run inside a transaction.
a.explanation: The transaction may be explicit or implicit.

b.text: The default isolation level is READ_COMMITTED.
b.explanation: Higher isolation can be achieved using explicit locks.

c.text: Secondaries must be updated synchronously before every commit.
c.explanation: Secondaries are replicated asynchronously.

d.text: Bookmarks can support read-your-writes or causal consistency.
d.explanation: They causally connect consecutive transactions.

graph-003.explanation: Neo4j combines ACID transactions with replicated cluster operation.

graph-004.prompt: Which Cypher pattern elements are interpreted correctly?

a.text: (p:Person) binds p to a node with the Person label.
a.explanation: It combines a variable and a label.

b.text: -[:ACTED_IN]-> requires a directed relationship of that type.
b.explanation: Both direction and type are specified.

c.text: -[r]- binds r to a relationship of any type and either direction.
c.explanation: There is no type or direction restriction.

d.text: () means that no node may exist at this position.
d.explanation: () matches any anonymous node.

graph-004.explanation: Anonymous pattern elements are matched but are not bound to variables.

graph-005.prompt: Which statements about relationship uniqueness within a Cypher pattern are correct?

a.text: The same stored relationship is not used twice for two pattern edges within one pattern.
a.explanation: In the co-actor example, this prevents matching the same ACTED_IN edge in both directions.

b.text: The same node may never occur twice in a pattern.
b.explanation: The restriction concerns relationships, not nodes in general.

c.text: Splitting a query into two MATCH clauses allows a relationship to be matched again in separate patterns.
c.explanation: Uniqueness applies per pattern.

d.text: The rule means that a relationship type may occur only once globally in a query.
d.explanation: Multiple different relationships of the same type are entirely valid.

graph-005.explanation: The rule concerns the identity of a relationship within one pattern.

graph-006.prompt: Which path lengths are allowed by the following Cypher expressions?

a.text: [:R*2] allows exactly two R relationships.
a.explanation: A single number without dots specifies a fixed length.

b.text: [:R*1..3] allows lengths 1, 2, and 3.
b.explanation: Both bounds are inclusive.

c.text: [:R*0..1] can also match only the starting node.
c.explanation: Length 0 is allowed.

d.text: [:R*..2] requires at least two relationships.
d.explanation: The upper bound is 2; the lower bound is not 2.

graph-006.explanation: Minimum and maximum hops describe relationship counts, not node counts.

graph-007.prompt: Which statements about path variables and shortest-path functions are correct?

a.text: nodes(p) returns the nodes of a path.
a.explanation: relationships(p) correspondingly returns its relationships.

b.text: length(p) counts the relationships in the path.
b.explanation: It does not count the nodes.

c.text: shortestPath returns one shortest path per match.
c.explanation: It does not necessarily return one global path across all target nodes.

d.text: allShortestPaths also returns longer alternatives that are not shortest.
d.explanation: It returns all equally short shortest paths.

graph-007.explanation: Path length and the number of paths are separate aspects.

graph-008.prompt: Given the following graph, which cities does the query return?
graph-008.context:
HAS_AIRPORT: Vienna→VIE, Paris→CDG, Berlin→BER, Berlin→TXL
FLIGHT: VIE→CDG, CDG→BER, VIE→TXL, TXL→VIE, BER→VIE

MATCH (:City {name:'Vienna'})-[:HAS_AIRPORT]->(:Airport)-[:FLIGHT*1..2]->(a:Airport),
      (c:City)-[:HAS_AIRPORT]->(a)
RETURN DISTINCT c.name

a.text: Paris
a.explanation: VIE→CDG is one hop.

b.text: Berlin
b.explanation: VIE→TXL is one hop, and VIE→CDG→BER is two hops.

c.text: Vienna
c.explanation: VIE→TXL→VIE is a two-hop path back to VIE.

d.text: No city, because variable-length paths cannot be combined with a second pattern.
d.explanation: The query is valid.

graph-008.explanation: The two-hop cycle causes the starting airport, and therefore Vienna, to be returned as well.

graph-009.prompt: Given the same graph, which (Airport,length) pairs does the query return?
graph-009.context:
FLIGHT: VIE→CDG, CDG→BER, VIE→TXL, TXL→VIE, BER→VIE

MATCH p = allShortestPaths((a:Airport)-[:FLIGHT*]->(:Airport {code:'BER'}))
WHERE a.code <> 'BER'
RETURN a.code, length(p)

a.text: CDG, 1
a.explanation: CDG→BER is direct.

b.text: VIE, 2
b.explanation: VIE→CDG→BER is the shortest path.

c.text: TXL, 3
c.explanation: TXL→VIE→CDG→BER.

d.text: BER, 0
d.explanation: The WHERE filter excludes a=BER.

graph-009.explanation: For each possible starting airport, the query considers its shortest directed FLIGHT connection to BER.

graph-010.prompt: Which statements correctly distinguish CREATE from MERGE?

a.text: CREATE creates the specified pattern even if an identical one already exists.
a.explanation: CREATE does not check for duplicates.

b.text: MERGE first attempts to match the complete specified pattern.
b.explanation: It creates the pattern only if matching fails.

c.text: MERGE (p:Person {name:'Alice', age:25}) must match an existing Alice node without an age property.
c.explanation: The complete property pattern does not match, so a new node may be created.

d.text: ON CREATE and ON MATCH can perform different SET actions.
d.explanation: This is an additional feature of MERGE.

graph-010.explanation: MERGE prevents duplicates only relative to the exact pattern it is supposed to match.

graph-011.prompt: A node (:Person {name:'Alice'}) exists without an age property. What can MERGE (p:Person {name:'Alice', age:25}) do?

a.text: It may create a second Person node named Alice with age=25.
a.explanation: The existing node does not match the complete property pattern.

b.text: It necessarily adds age=25 to the existing Alice node.
b.explanation: MERGE does not automatically patch a partial match.

c.text: A uniqueness constraint on Person.name could prevent creation of the second node and raise an error.
c.explanation: The new node would violate the unique name constraint.

d.text: MERGE ignores all properties while matching.
d.explanation: Properties in the MERGE pattern are part of the match.

graph-011.explanation: For controlled updates, it is often safer to MERGE on a unique key and use a separate SET.

graph-012.prompt: Which statements about SET and DELETE are correct?

a.text: SET p.age = null removes the age property.
a.explanation: This is equivalent to REMOVE p.age.

b.text: DELETE can remove a node with existing relationships without any further action.
b.explanation: The relationships must be deleted first, or DETACH DELETE must be used.

c.text: DETACH DELETE removes a node and its incoming and outgoing relationships.
c.explanation: That is exactly its effect.

d.text: A relationship can be deleted directly with DELETE.
d.explanation: After matching the relationship, DELETE r is valid.

graph-012.explanation: By default, node deletion protects against orphaned relationships.

graph-013.prompt: Which statements about Cypher aggregation and WITH are correct?

a.text: Non-aggregated RETURN fields implicitly form the grouping.
a.explanation: An explicit GROUP BY is not required.

b.text: count(expr) also counts NULL values of expr.
b.explanation: count(expr) counts only non-NULL values.

c.text: WITH can name an aggregate and filter it with WHERE in the next query part.
c.explanation: For example: WITH p,count(*) AS n WHERE n>5.

d.text: collect() creates a list of values.
d.explanation: It is a Cypher aggregation function.

graph-013.explanation: WITH is the pipeline boundary for multi-stage aggregation and filtering.

graph-014.prompt: Which queries correctly answer: 'List cities with at least one airport from which a direct TRAIN relationship leads to another airport'?

a.text: MATCH (c:City)-[:HAS_AIRPORT]->(:Airport)-[:TRAIN]->(:Airport) RETURN DISTINCT c.name
a.explanation: The pattern requires exactly the requested outgoing TRAIN relationship.

b.text: MATCH (c:City)-[:HAS_AIRPORT]->(a:Airport) WHERE EXISTS { (a)-[:TRAIN]->(:Airport) } RETURN DISTINCT c.name
b.explanation: The existence subquery expresses the same condition.

c.text: MATCH (c:City)-[:HAS_AIRPORT]->(:Airport)<-[:TRAIN]-(:Airport) RETURN DISTINCT c.name
c.explanation: This requires an incoming TRAIN relationship to the city's airport.

d.text: MATCH (:Airport)-[:TRAIN]->(a:Airport)<-[:HAS_AIRPORT]-(c:City) RETURN DISTINCT c.name
d.explanation: This also lists destinations of incoming TRAIN relationships, not their origin cities.

graph-014.explanation: For directed relationships, arrow direction determines the origin and destination city.

column-001.prompt: Which statements explain the advantage of classical column stores for analytics?

a.text: A query can read only the columns it actually needs.
a.explanation: This reduces I/O for wide tables.

b.text: Values in one column share the same data type and often have similar distributions.
b.explanation: This improves compression and processing.

c.text: SIMD and good cache locality fit column-wise processing.
c.explanation: The same operation is applied to many values.

d.text: Every single-row insertion automatically becomes cheaper than in a row store.
d.explanation: Updates across separate, compressed columns can be expensive.

column-001.explanation: Column stores optimize selective analytical scans, not every workload in general.

column-002.prompt: Which statements about explicit and virtual IDs are correct?

a.text: Explicit IDs store a tuple ID with every column value.
a.explanation: This allows values from the same row to be connected.

b.text: Explicit IDs increase data volume and I/O.
b.explanation: The IDs are stored with every column.

c.text: Virtual IDs can use a value's position as its identity.
c.explanation: The offset replaces a separate ID column.

d.text: Variable-width compression always makes virtual IDs easier.
d.explanation: It makes direct offset calculation more difficult.

column-002.explanation: Virtual IDs save space but require stable, computable positions.

column-003.prompt: Which statements about run-length encoding are correct?

a.text: It stores sequences of equal values as (value, repetition count).
a.explanation: This is the RLE principle.

b.text: It works especially well for long consecutive repetitions.
b.explanation: Many entries can then be represented by one pair.

c.text: For alternating or almost entirely unique values, it can be worse than uncompressed storage.
c.explanation: The metadata for every short run adds overhead.

d.text: It necessarily preserves a directly computable fixed byte position for every original value.
d.explanation: Variable run lengths make virtual offsets harder to compute.

column-003.explanation: RLE depends heavily on the order of the values.

column-004.prompt: Which mappings between data and compression methods are plausible?

a.text: Long sequences of the same invoice date → Run-Length Encoding
a.explanation: Long runs are ideal for RLE.

b.text: Birth month with only 12 possible values → Bit-Vector Encoding
b.explanation: A small domain is suitable.

c.text: Long recurring address strings → Dictionary Encoding
c.explanation: Long values are replaced with short IDs.

d.text: A column containing almost only NULL → Null Suppression
d.explanation: Sparse values are stored without the many NULLs.

column-004.explanation: All mappings correspond to typical use cases of the methods.

column-005.prompt: Which general statements about the size comparison of RLE, bit-vector, and dictionary encoding are correct?

a.text: RLE can be smaller or larger than bit-vector encoding, depending on the data.
a.explanation: Long runs favor RLE, while frequent changes may favor bitmaps.

b.text: Dictionary encoding is always smaller than RLE for every possible column.
b.explanation: No method dominates every data distribution.

c.text: Bit-vector encoding can require enormous space when there are very many distinct values.
c.explanation: It generally needs one bit string per value.

d.text: The concrete value distribution and ordering are decisive.
d.explanation: Compression choices are column- and application-specific.

column-005.explanation: Without concrete data, comparisons usually do not yield an unambiguous ranking.

column-006.prompt: Which statements about operations on compressed data are correct?

a.text: A SUM over RLE data can multiply values by their repetition counts.
a.explanation: A full expansion is not required.

b.text: Direct processing can save I/O and decompression costs.
b.explanation: This is the ideal performance gain.

c.text: Every arbitrary operation automatically works on every compression format.
c.explanation: The query engine needs method-specific implementations.

d.text: Compressed execution can make the system more complex.
d.explanation: Many formats and operator variants must be supported.

column-006.explanation: Compression and query execution can be integrated, but not for free.

column-007.prompt: Which statements correctly distinguish early from late materialization?

a.text: Early materialization assembles rows shortly after reading the columns.
a.explanation: This is early reconstruction.

b.text: Late materialization works with individual columns and position lists for as long as possible.
b.explanation: This preserves column-oriented advantages.

c.text: Early materialization typically promotes further vectorization and compression benefits.
c.explanation: It loses many of these benefits early.

d.text: Some aggregations can avoid full materialization entirely.
d.explanation: If only filtered values from one column are aggregated, position references are sufficient.

column-007.explanation: Materialization is needed when attributes must be combined, but its timing is crucial.

a.text: Position 2
a.explanation: Ra[2]=16 lies within the inclusive interval.

b.text: Position 4
b.explanation: Ra[4]=9 qualifies.

c.text: Position 5
c.explanation: Ra[5]=5 lies on the inclusive lower bound.

d.text: Position 3
d.explanation: Ra[3]=25 is greater than 20.

column-008.explanation: select returns position IDs, not the values themselves.

column-009.prompt: Execute the late-materialization plan mentally. Which intermediate results are correct?

a.explanation: The values 16, 9, and 5 qualify.

b.explanation: Rb is reconstructed at the three positions.

c.explanation: reverse swaps value and pos.

d.explanation: Rb=56 at position 4 has no match in Sa.

column-010.prompt: Continue the plan from the previous question. Which statements are correct?

a.text: inter3 = [2,5]
a.explanation: voidTail keeps the left position.

b.explanation: Ra is reconstructed only for the surviving R positions.

c.text: result = 21

d.text: Complete R and S tuples must additionally be materialized for the result.
d.explanation: The aggregation needs only positions and the required Ra column.

column-010.explanation: The example shows how late materialization avoids unnecessary reconstruction.

column-011.prompt: Which statements about updates in column stores are correct?

a.text: An insert can require writes to several separate column files.
a.explanation: Every attribute of the new row must be added.

b.text: An update in a compressed column can cause decompression and recompression.
b.explanation: In the worst case, the structure must be rebuilt.

c.text: A separate write store can accept new changes in an update-friendly form.
c.explanation: The large read store remains optimized.

d.text: Queries may ignore the write store until it has been merged into the read store.
d.explanation: Queries must evaluate both stores and combine the results.

column-011.explanation: Read and write stores separate optimized layouts but require combined query evaluation.

a.text: Bigtable can be understood as a map from row key, column, and timestamp to a value.
a.explanation: This is the central data model.

b.text: Rows are stored lexicographically by their keys.
b.explanation: The key choice therefore controls data locality.

c.text: A tablet is the unit of distribution and load balancing.
c.explanation: It groups consecutive rows.

d.text: Transactions across arbitrarily many rows are the primary guaranteed atomicity boundary.
d.explanation: Access within a single row is atomic.

column-012.explanation: Bigtable is a distributed, sparse, versioned wide-column map.

column-013.prompt: Which statements about Bigtable column families and versions are correct?

a.text: Column families must be created explicitly before data can be stored.
a.explanation: They form the stable schema core.

b.text: A family can contain a very large number of qualifiers.
b.explanation: The number of columns in a family is practically unbounded.

c.text: Cell versions are stored in descending timestamp order.
c.explanation: The newest versions come first.

d.text: Garbage collection can be configured only globally for the entire table.
d.explanation: Retention options are specified per column family.

column-013.explanation: Families are coarse, explicit groups; qualifiers and versions provide flexible fine-grained structure.

column-014.prompt: Which statements about Bigtable infrastructure and related systems are correct?

a.text: GFS stores log and data files.
a.explanation: It is the distributed file-system foundation.

b.text: SSTables are immutable files sorted by key and equipped with a block index.
b.explanation: They are a central persistent format.

c.text: Chubby is a distributed lock service and an entry point for locating Bigtable data.
c.explanation: It coordinates the system and helps clients find it.

d.text: According to the slides, HBase and Cassandra have identical master architectures and PACELC classifications.
d.explanation: HBase is master-based PC/EC; Cassandra is masterless PA/EL.

column-014.explanation: Wide-column systems share ideas but make different architecture and consistency decisions.
